summary: Gruber offers a comprehensive analysis of Apple's October 2013 event, covering the iPad Air, Retina iPad Mini, free Mavericks and iWork, and the new Mac Pro. He argues the iPad Air and Retina Mini represent a convergence into two sizes of essentially the same device, with the Mini's achievement of technical parity being a year ahead of expectations. He defends Apple's event format against complaints of staleness, explains how free software squeezes Microsoft from a different angle than Google, and addresses iWork's controversial feature regressions as a deliberate tradeoff for cross-platform parity. He closes with a nuanced take on the Tim Cook era, suggesting iOS 7 represents a genuine departure from Jobs's taste while the hardware advances would have made Jobs proud.
key_points: The iPad Air and Retina Mini are now essentially the same device in two sizes, making the choice between them harder than ever and setting a template Gruber hopes Apple follows with a larger iPhone
Making Mavericks and iWork free squeezes Microsoft alongside Google: Apple gives away software to sell hardware, Google gives it away to sell ads, leaving less room for selling software alone
iWork's Mac feature regressions were a deliberate sacrifice for cross-platform parity across iPhone, iPad, web, and Mac — a tradeoff no other office suite has achieved

The Retina iPad Mini achieving A7 parity with the Air in one year, with negligible size/weight increases, is primarily a triumph of operational efficiency and chip design rather than industrial design
iOS 7 is a tangible sign that Tim Cook's Apple will diverge from what Jobs would have done, while the hardware advances represent continuity with Jobs's vision
money_quotes: If you want a new Apple event, you're going to have to wait for a new Apple product.
Apple gives away software for free in exchange for your buying their hardware. This is not charity.
Apple and Google are squeezing Microsoft from both sides, and the result is that less and less perceived value in the industry resides solely in software.
Last year's Mini was a triumph of design; this year's update is a triumph of operational efficiency.
No other office platform in the world has that — complete parity between native apps for phone, tablet, desktop, and a web app.
The A7 is an 'only Apple could do this' piece of technology, and Jobs would have exulted in it.
I don't think I'm saying anything here we haven't all thought, regardless of what we each think of the iOS 7 look and feel individually.
I doubt the Mac Pro is the only product Apple wants to assemble like this.
key_insight: The 2013 iPad lineup represents Apple's operational excellence converging two product lines into near-identical devices at two sizes, a feat driven more by chip design and supply chain mastery than industrial design, while free software strategically squeezes competitors who depend on software revenue.
